The latency patterns that only appear under concurrent load, and how to find them before users do.

12 min read
Just now

--

Every backend engineer has lived this story at least once.
The API endpoint is fast in development. Fast in staging. Fast in load testing with ten concurrent users. The team is confident. The deployment goes to production.
Two weeks later, the support tickets start. Users are complaining that the endpoint is slow. You pull up the staging environment and test it. Fast. You test it in production with a single request. Fast. You watch the production metrics during low traffic. Fast.
You watch the production metrics during peak traffic. Slow.
The endpoint is not slow. The endpoint under concurrent production load is slow. Those are different problems and they require different investigations.
The gap between them is one of the most consistent sources of production latency that engineering teams encounter and one of the least systematically addressed in standard load testing practices.
This is what lives in that gap and how to find it.

Why Staging Lies About Production Performance
Staging environments are useful for functional correctness. They are systematically misleading about production performance.
The misleading is not accidental. It is structural. Staging environments differ from production in the exact ways that matter most for latency under concurrent load.
The first difference is data volume. Staging databases typically contain a fraction of the data that production databases contain. Query performance that scales with data volume looks correct in staging and slow in production. An index that performs well on ten thousand rows may not perform well on ten million. A query that returns results quickly from a small table may return results slowly from a large one. A join that is efficient on a staging dataset may trigger a full table scan on the production dataset because the query planner’s statistics suggest a different execution plan is optimal.
The second difference is concurrency. Staging environments typically see a small number of concurrent users. Load tests with ten or twenty concurrent users do not reveal the failure modes that appear at five hundred concurrent users. Connection pool exhaustion, lock contention, cache stampedes, and thread starvation all require sufficient concurrency to trigger. Staging tests that don’t reach that concurrency threshold tell you that the endpoint is correct, not that it is fast under real conditions.
The third difference is cache state. Production caches are warm. Frequently accessed data is in memory. Staging caches are often cold. The response time in staging includes the database lookup that production avoids through caching. A staging test that shows a response time of two hundred milliseconds might be showing a response time that production users only see on the first request after a cache miss. Normal production users see fifty milliseconds because the cache is warm.
These three differences combine to produce staging measurements that are systematically optimistic about production latency. The endpoint that takes fifty milliseconds in staging and two hundred milliseconds in production is not broken. It is operating correctly in both environments. The environments are different in ways that produce different performance.
The investigation that treats the production latency as a bug will look for broken code. The investigation that treats it as an environmental difference will look for the specific differences that account for the latency gap.
The Concurrency Patterns That Only Appear in Production
There are four latency patterns that require concurrent load to manifest and are therefore invisible in standard staging tests.
The first is connection pool contention.
A connection pool has a maximum size. Under low concurrency, requests get connections immediately. Under high concurrency, requests queue for connections. The queue time is pure latency that adds directly to end-to-end response time.
The staging environment with ten concurrent users never exhausts the connection pool. The production environment with five hundred concurrent users exhausts it regularly during peak traffic.
The signature in production: response time that increases as concurrent users increase, even though individual query times remain constant. The queries are not slower. The wait for a connection is adding latency before the query even starts.
The diagnostic metric: connection pool wait time, not query time. These are different measurements. Query time measures how long the database takes to execute the query. Wait time measures how long the application waited for a connection before sending the query.
Most monitoring setups track query time. Connection pool wait time requires specific instrumentation. HikariCP exposes this metric. It is almost never on the primary dashboard until someone has investigated a production latency problem and discovered that query time was fine while wait time was the cause.
The fix is almost never “make the queries faster.” The fix is pool sizing relative to concurrent load, query duration reduction to release connections faster, or connection leak elimination.
The second concurrency pattern is lock contention at scale.
Individual transactions that each take fifty milliseconds produce negligible lock contention at low concurrency. The same transactions at high concurrency produce significant lock contention because multiple transactions are competing for locks on the same rows or tables simultaneously.
The staging environment with ten concurrent users has a low probability of two transactions competing for the same lock at the same time. The production environment with five hundred concurrent users has a high probability. The same code produces different lock contention levels at different concurrency levels.
The signature in production: response time for specific operations that increases disproportionately during peak traffic compared to off-peak traffic. The operations that are affected are the ones that modify shared data. Read operations are rarely affected because reads typically don’t acquire exclusive locks.
The diagnostic is pg_stat_activity filtered for lock wait events during peak traffic. The transactions waiting for locks reveal which rows or tables are contended, which reveals which operations are competing.
The fix requires understanding the locking pattern and either reducing lock duration, changing the lock type, or restructuring the operations to reduce contention.
The third concurrency pattern is cache stampede.
A cache stampede happens when a cache entry expires and multiple concurrent requests simultaneously discover the miss and all attempt to populate the cache from the database. Instead of one request doing the expensive database lookup and all subsequent requests hitting the cache, many requests do the expensive lookup simultaneously.
At low concurrency, cache misses are rare events. A single request sees the miss, fetches from the database, populates the cache. The next request hits the cache. The miss is invisible in the latency metrics because it affects one request at a time.
At high concurrency, when a popular cache entry expires, dozens of requests simultaneously see the miss. Dozens of expensive database queries fire simultaneously. The database is suddenly receiving a load spike from cache misses rather than serving cached responses. The response time for those requests is the database query time, not the cache hit time.
The signature in production: periodic latency spikes that are correlated with cache expiration patterns. If cache TTLs are set to one hour, latency spikes appear approximately every hour on popular cache keys.
The diagnostic is correlating latency spikes with cache miss rate spikes. If the miss rate spikes and the latency spikes occur at the same time, cache stampede is the cause.
The fix is a probabilistic early expiration strategy that proactively refreshes cache entries before they expire, or a single-flight pattern that allows only one concurrent request to populate a cache entry while others wait for the result.
The fourth concurrency pattern is thread starvation.
A thread pool processes requests. Each thread handles one request at a time. If requests block threads for longer than expected, the thread pool fills. New requests queue waiting for an available thread.
Under low concurrency, the thread pool is never fully utilized. Under high concurrency, a slow operation that blocks threads can fill the pool and cause all subsequent requests to queue.
The slow operation does not need to be the operation being measured. If a notification is sent synchronously after every user action, and the notification service becomes slow, the thread processing the user action is blocked while waiting for the notification. The thread pool fills with threads waiting for notification responses. All user actions queue, not just the ones where the notification is slow.
The signature is broad latency increase across multiple endpoints that coincides with a specific downstream service becoming slow. The latency is not in the endpoint’s own processing. It is in the thread blocking on a slow downstream call.
The diagnostic is tracing which downstream calls are blocking threads during peak latency. Distributed tracing infrastructure makes this visible by showing the time spent in each downstream call for every request.
The fix is making the blocking operation asynchronous, adding a circuit breaker that returns quickly when the downstream service is slow, or reducing the timeout to limit how long a thread blocks.
The Data Volume Patterns That Emerge in Production
Beyond concurrency, data volume produces its own category of latency patterns that staging environments systematically hide.
The N+1 problem at scale is the most common.
An endpoint that fetches a list of items and then fetches associated data for each item executes one query per item in the list. With ten items, this is eleven queries. The performance is acceptable.
Production datasets grow. The same endpoint now fetches lists of five hundred items. Five hundred and one queries. Each query takes one millisecond. The total is five hundred milliseconds from database queries alone.
The staging environment with a small dataset showed an eleven-millisecond response time. The production environment with a large dataset shows a five-hundred-millisecond response time. Nothing changed in the code. The data grew.
The diagnostic approach is measuring query count per request, not query time. An endpoint that executes more queries when it returns more data is exhibiting N+1 behavior.
The fix is eager loading the associated data in a single query rather than one query per item. In ORM frameworks, this is the difference between lazy loading and eager loading, or between a standard find query and a find query with a join or includes clause.
Query plan regression at scale is a more subtle problem.
The database query planner uses statistics about data distribution to choose execution plans. The statistics are collected by the autovacuum process. The planner’s choice of execution plan depends on the statistics being accurate.
When data distribution changes significantly and the statistics lag behind, the planner makes suboptimal choices. An index that should be used is not used because the planner estimates a full table scan would be cheaper based on stale statistics. A join that should be ordered one way is ordered differently because the planner estimates incorrect row counts.
The staging environment with a small and static dataset rarely experiences plan regression because the statistics are accurate and the planner’s choices are optimal. The production environment with a large and changing dataset experiences plan regression when the statistics become stale.
